title: Qantas shares fall more than 6 per cent after first-half profit announced
text:
Qantas shares have lost more than 6 per cent in value after the airline reported a flat first-half result, with statutory profit after tax of $925 million, just $2 million higher than the same time last year.

The airline's sales rose by 3 per cent in the last six months, while pre-tax earnings per share of 61 cents were below market expectations of closer to 66 cents.

Its fully franked dividend of 19.8 cents per share was also below expectations.

The airline has reported underlying profit before tax of $1.4 billion, up $71 million on the same period last year (up 5.1 per cent).

Qantas profit leaps 28 per cent to $1.6 billion Qantas reports a 28 per cent rise in net profit as boss Vanessa Hudson says the company will "learn from" the fallout of its unlawful outsourcing of ground handling.

This morning, shareholders initially reacted positively to Qantas' first-half profit result, with the company's share price jumping 4 per cent, but that early enthusiasm quickly faded.

A little after midday, its share price was trading around $9.98 a share (-6.34 per cent).

"The market has struggled to digest the misses on underlying profit before tax and statutory net profit after tax (both below consensus), combined with management highlighting rising airport charges, government fees, and other costs outpacing inflation — raising questions about future margin sustainability," IG analyst Tony Sycamore said in a note.

Statutory profit includes costs attributed to Jetstar Asia's closure, organisational restructuring, management of a cyber incident, legal penalties, and employee ownership plan awards.

Qantas touts ongoing fleet renewal

When announcing the profit result this morning, Qantas Group chief executive Vanessa Hudson said the airline is still investing in the largest fleet renewal in its history, which has helped to drive the group's recent financial performance.

"Around 60 per cent of Jetstar's increase in profitability in the half was driven by its new aircraft," she said.

"This gives us confidence in the benefits that will flow once Qantas' new aircraft reach scale.

Vanessa Hudson says Qantas' new aircraft are helping to drive the group's financial performance. (ABC News: John Gunn)

"We've already started to see an acceleration in deliveries for Qantas, with six new aircraft arriving in the first half and a further 30 arriving over the next 18 months.

"Some of these new aircraft will replace older aircraft, while some will support growth by opening up new routes, like the ultra-long range A350s, which will operate Project Sunrise flights.

"[We] are also increasing returns to shareholders. The Board has approved a $300 million fully franked base dividend, an increase of $50 million, along with a [$150 million] share buyback," she said.

Penalties paid for illegal outsourcing, state of US economy being monitored

Qantas' operating cash flow was down 15 per cent (-$321 million) to $1.75 billion in the half, year-on-year.

That was due to increased tax outflows and $90 million in legal penalties for illegally outsourcing its ground handling workforce during the COVID pandemic.

Qantas fined $90 million for illegally outsourcing jobs The airline giant will face a $90 million penalty over its decision to illegally outsource more than 1,800 ground handling jobs during the COVID pandemic.

The airline said the "gross impact" of the Albanese government's "Same Job, Same Pay" laws on the airline's wage costs in financial year 2026 was approximately $95 million, but that impact was "expected to mitigate over time".

Looking forward, the airline said it expected strong travel demand across its portfolio, with caveats.

"The evolving economic environment in the US will continue to be monitored," it said.

Qantas' Group Domestic unit delivered $1.05 billion in underlying EBIT (earnings before interest and taxes), up 14 per cent. Its domestic revenue grew by 5 per cent, supported by a 4 per cent increase in capacity.

Six new aircraft arrived in the half, including A321XLRs, which entered service in September. It says that as more aircraft enter the fleet, they will enable the planned retirement of the airline's older Boeing 737s, beginning in late calendar year 2026.

It says QantasLink also benefited from fleet renewal, with more mod-life A320 and A319 aircraft supporting 10 per cent capacity growth across intra-WA.

Jetstar a stand-out

Jetstar's domestic operations beat expectations.

Jetstar carried more than 8.5 million passengers in the half, which helped it deliver a 38 per cent increase in underlying EBIT, with more than half of Jetstar's customers flying for less than $150.

'Flow-on delays' possible as Jetstar services resume after Airbus issue The airline is warning further disruptions are possible after the issue sparked widespread delays across Australia on Saturday.

Jetstar's international business performed strongly, with earnings from its Australian international airline up 9 per cent.

It says the delivery of two more A321LRs during the period enabled increased flying on popular routes and the launch of new routes, including Newcastle-Denpasar, Gold Coast-Denpasar, Brisbane-Cebu and Perth-Manila.

Singapore-based Jetstar Asia closed in July, and the group has announced its intention to sell its stake in Jetstar Japan as it focuses on its core business in Australia.

Qantas loyalty program

The airline says its loyalty business delivered underlying EBIT of $286 million, up 12 per cent.

Qantas Frequent Flyer grew to over 18.3 million people. Points earned through retail partners increased by almost 20 per cent, with long-term partners Woolworths and Red Energy seeing double-digit growth.

Its partnership with David Jones launched in September.

Global ranking reveals poor value for Aussie airline loyalty programs Millions of Australians are signed up to airline loyalty schemes, but a new global ranking finds travellers are getting poorer value than from overseas carriers.

It says points earned across financial services grew by 6 per cent, further supported by diversification, including Qantas Money home loans, with the portfolio now exceeding $2 billion in value.

Points redeemed grew by 17 per cent, and more than 2.5 million reward seats were booked in the six-month period, an average of 14,000 a day.

It says retail partnership redemptions were up 15 per cent, with Ticketek redemptions rising by 50 per cent as members used points for sporting and music events.

title: Hearing reveals panic at Optus call centre as Triple Zero crisis unfolded
text:
There has been bombshell testimony about Optus's processes at a Senate committee hearing.

In the early hours of September 18 last year, and for the following 14 hours, 605 Optus customers sought Triple Zero (000) emergency services and assistance of some kind.

Most of those callers were met with silence on the other end of the phone.

Kerry Schott AO, the experienced executive tasked with reviewing what happened and why, has fronted a Senate Environment and Communications References Committee.

The hearing's terms of reference include the cause of the outage, including the performance of emergency "camp-on" arrangements, and the role of the minister for communications and the Australian government in safeguarding the integrity, resilience and public confidence of the Triple Zero system.

It will also look at the impact on Optus customers, including in relation to the tragic deaths of a number of people as a result of not being able to telephone Triple Zero for emergency assistance.

The committee heard families who failed to reach Triple Zero were highly distressed. (ABC News: Callum Flinn)

The report titled The Triple Zero Outage At Optus: 18 September 2025 has been published.

It revealed numerous errors by Optus, both in the implementation of the network upgrade and the risk management procedures the telco had in place to troubleshoot the problem.

'Distressing' calls

Ms Schott described to senators the "particularly distressing" experience of listening to the calls made by families, who had tried and failed to reach the Triple Zero service, to the offshore Optus call centre.

"They had managed to get through to Triple Zero on some other line or call, but they were actually ringing to alert Optus that there was a problem with Triple Zero and all of them described the problem accurately, which was they couldn't get through and all they had on their mobile was silence," Ms Schott said.

"They had been through considerable personal distress and were basically doing a public service by ringing up to say what was wrong.

"And then to have people trying to discuss with them a technical fault at that time, the calls are just terrible to listen to."

Reports not upwardly referred

The committee heard that central to the escalation of the Triple Zero outage crisis was a lack of awareness by call centre staff that a major problem was unfolding.

"[The call centre staff] hadn't been told [by upper management] that there was an outage," Ms Schott told the committee.

"If they had been told there was an outage, they would have treated the matter differently.

Most of the 605 Optus customers who called Triple Zero on September 18 last year were met with silence on the other end of the phone. (ABC News)

"But they were, from where they were sitting, they were just getting an odd call about somebody who was having trouble getting through.

"So, in a sense, they just took it as a troubleshooting or a technical call rather than an emergency situation and a serious problem."

This is the reason, Ms Schott put forward, that call centre staff did not upwardly refer the calls they were receiving.

"Later, when they finally did get round to referring the issue, it was following the knowledge that there were fatalities, that they were aware of two fatalities," she said.

"And at that time, they were extraordinarily distressed and the senior people at the call centre actually seemed to spend an hour or more just calming everyone down."

title: Hundreds join support group in wake of claims against gynaecologist
text:
More former patients of Melbourne gynaecologist Simon Gordon have come forward to express distress over their treatment with many joining a newly-formed private support group.

A Four Corners investigation revealed Dr Gordon's patients were repeatedly operated on for what they were told was severe endometriosis despite that diagnosis not being seen in pathology results.

Sarah Hayden, 51, told the ABC she has had endometriosis since she was a teenager and received multiple recommendations to see Dr Gordon who was often referred to as "the best of the best".

Years of complaints about surgeon 'swept under the carpet' Complaints had been made about this gynaecologist for years. Only when hospital management discovered that Four Corners was looking into his practice, did it start investigating further.

Ms Hayden said she was surprised and shocked to discover after a laparoscopy surgery that one of her ovaries and fallopian tubes had been removed.

"I ended up spending a couple of days in hospital because I wasn't able to use my bladder and the pain was so bad that we couldn't get it under control," she said.

"It ended up being a pretty traumatic surgery experience as opposed to my previous laparoscopies where I was in and out in the same day."

Ms Hayden said since the surgery she has experienced severe pain.

"I've had more excruciating pain on a daily basis than I ever had from endo prior to that surgery," she said.

"And interestingly most of that pain comes from exactly where my ovary and fallopian tube were.

Help for endometriosis sufferers If allegations that a surgeon performed surgery for "severe" endometriosis on women with little or no sign of the disease have you questioning your own treatment, here is where you can voice your concerns.

"I get the most agonising daily sort of nerve pain shooting into my hips and legs and deep into my abdomen."

Ms Hayden said discovering other former patients had similar experiences left her with a mix of emotions.

"I think so many of us are feeling the same, a mixture of grief and devastation, absolute rage and fury but also just this sense of relief that I'm not the only one.

"It wasn't just my experience, I'm not crazy and the unbelievable thing is so many women's stories are the exact same," she said.

Ms Hayden said she and another former patient had used social media to set up a private support group and more than 200 women joined in just two days.

Sarah Hayden says she was left in agony after surgery performed by Dr Simon Gordon. (Supplied)

"I've got probably 300 to 400 inboxes on Instagram and Facebook… and most of the inboxes are things like, 'I'm absolutely devastated, I don't know what to do, I was made to have a hysterectomy, I couldn't have children, I was told that I had to have it'," she said.

The ABC's Four Corners investigation found there had been multiple complaints by clinicians and patients about Dr Gordon.

Why this week has left endometriosis sufferers reeling Endometriosis sufferers have been left "sick" and reeling over the findings of a months-long ABC investigation into a Melbourne gynaecologist.

The Victorian Government has now referred the allegations against Dr Gordon to police.

Premier Jacinta Allan said victims deserved justice.

"Without commenting on the specific case, let me be clear: performing unnecessary surgery is a crime, removing women's organs without a clinical need is a crime and assisting in that conduct is a crime," she said.

Dr Gordon maintains he always acted ethically and responsibly and told the ABC he, "never performed surgery to treat endometriosis or any other condition unless I was absolutely convinced it was in the patient's best interest and to improve their overall quality of life."

title: Town learns its water is 'only good to flush the toilet' with after 66 years
text:
The first time Darren Clifton learnt the water he had been showering and cleaning his teeth with for years was not actually safe, was by accident.

Mr Clifton had owned his property in the far-west New South Wales community of Sunset Strip for five years when a letter from water utility Essential Water was circulated on social media — but it was not shared by the utility.

It was from a new arrival to town.

Darren Clifton (right) and Joseph Cassar (left) both found out from social media that their water was not safe to wash in. (ABC Broken Hill: Coquohalla Connor)

Mr Clifton, who had lived in the district his whole life, was caught completely by surprise.

"The letter posted on Facebook … virtually deemed the water as unfit for any human use whatsoever," he said.

"I clean my teeth in it every day, I shower in it every day.

" [But] from what we've just found out, it's only good to flush the toilet. "

The letter from Essential Water says boiling is not enough to make the tap water safe. (Supplied: Facebook)

Despite Mr Clifton receiving a monthly water bill from Essential Water, he said there was never any communication from the company to inform him his water was "unsuitable for drinking or food consumption" and "not safe for showering, bathing and washing activities" — as was stated in the letter shared online.

"[There was] no verbal correspondence or written correspondence regarding the quality of the water and what it can be used for," he said.

Mr Clifton said he was not alone in being caught unaware of the water guidelines.

The lakeside community 20km from Menindee was established in 1960. (ABC Broken Hill: Coquohalla Connor)

While Sunset Strip residents knew the water was not safe to drink, he said people had been showering and bathing in it for 66 years, ever since Sunset Strip was established.

It has left Mr Clifton and other residents questioning what they have been paying for.

Some also now fear what they have been exposed to.

New home owner 'absolutely disgusted'

Before buying their Sunset Strip home last year, Joseph Cassar and his partner had contacted the water supplier, Essential Water, to make sure it had town water connectivity.

"We bought the place based on that information," Mr Cassar said.

Sunset Strip resident Joseph Cassar bought his house a year ago but only just learnt his water is not safe for washing or cleaning. (ABC Broken Hill: Coquohalla Connor)

It was only after they arrived in town that they started to hear about the non-potable nature of the tap water, and that they would have to buy drinking water.

A new resident received the notice in a "Welcome pack" from Essential Water. (Supplied: Facebook)

This was then confirmed to him by Essential Water, which he says was not flagged when he made his initial enquiries prior to buying his house.

However, the real "shock" came this month when the couple saw the letter on Facebook — shared by a new arrival — warning them the water was unfit for bathing or washing of any kind.

"I couldn't believe it when I saw this letter," Mr Cassar said.

" I was absolutely disgusted. "

They soon learnt the town's long-term residents were equally taken aback by the news.

The small community on Lake Menindee was caught completely unaware by its unsafe water. (ABC Broken Hill: Lily McCure)

"Everyone knew that it was non-potable, but not that they couldn't shower or wash dishes or clothes in it," Mr Cassar said.

"If I knew the water situation was as bad as it is, I don't think we would have purchased this place.

"That would have been a make-or-break situation."

Long-term residents 'gobsmacked'

Ms Holmes says up until last week the only warning signs she'd seen state the water is undrinkable. (Supplied: Kat Holmes)

Kat Holmes has owned her property at Sunset Strip for 15 years and, like Mr Cassar and Mr Clifton, the letter that was shared to social media was the first time she had heard about the water quality.

Ms Holmes added that when she first moved to the remote town on the northern banks of Lake Menindee, she was not informed that the water was not safe for drinking.

"I was living there for two years before I found out [that it was non-potable]," she said.

"We were drinking it; I was giving it to my grandchildren."

Ms Holmes eventually learnt the water was not safe to drink but was told it was fine for other uses.

"When I rang Essential Water 13 years ago, they told me it was safe to brush my teeth in it — no more toxic than the toothpaste I was using," she said.

Since then, she has spent thousands of dollars on rainwater tanks and filtration systems to secure safe drinking water.

The water treatment plant at Sunset Strip was commissioned in 2004. (ABC Broken Hill: Coquohalla Connor)

So, when she too saw the letter advising against showering or washing, she was "gobsmacked".

"There are over 130 houses up here. All these people need to shower, they need to wash their clothes, their dishes," Ms Holmes said.

"Now they've said no washing of any kind — it worries me greatly."

Water provider sorry for 'inconsistent' communication

The Sunset Strip water is piped from the Murray River and is chlorinated and filtered, but not to the standard of Australian drinking water guidelines and therefore cannot be classified as potable.

Essential Water head Ross Berry acknowledged there had been "confusing" and "inconsistent" messaging in recent times.

Ross Berry says Essential Water is required to communicate that the water they supply is not suitable for showering or washing. (ABC Broken Hill: Oliver Brown)

"We haven't been consistent and we'd like to apologise to the people of Sunset Strip," Mr Berry said.

He said due to the nature of the supply, the utility must communicate that it was not fit for showering or washing, though customers ultimately decided how they used it.

The ABC has reached out to the Central Darling Shire Council for comment.

It is understood there are no current plans to pipe potable drinking water to Sunset Strip or upgrade the current treatment plant facilities.

title: The AFL rule change which has sparked fears of an injury influx in 2026
text:
Shannon Neale's ruck injury scare could prompt some clubs to reconsider which big men they throw into centre ball-ups amid the AFL's rule changes for the position.

Neale gave the Cats a massive scare when he clashed knees with Carlton ruckman Marc Pittonet in last night's preseason match.

The athletic forward, who kicked 44 goals last year, was in serious pain holding his right knee, with many onlookers and pundits fearing he had injured his posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) due to the knee-on-knee contact.

But the Cats later confirmed Neale had suffered a shin knock and were confident he would be "fine" for next Friday's opening round clash with Gold Coast.

When asked if it made him consider who went in those ruck contests, Cats assistant James Kelly said: "Yeah, probably.

"How you deploy your athletic guys is always a question across the AFL.

"I know that there are teams that don't like to put key forwards in there. We're generally not one of those. The flexibility within our team is something we value.

"I can't speak for other teams, but I think for us … we like having guys in there that can jump and can run. But I'm sure some teams will certainly think about it."

The Neale-Pittonet knee clash happened just days after Melbourne captain Max Gawn expressed concerns about how the new ruck rules, which encourage jumping over wrestling, could lead to more PCL injuries.

"Hopefully, if they're up vertical and not in, that could maybe prevent the PCL — because that's the one we're probably worried about," Carlton backline coach Ash Hansen said.

"But I guess time will tell — that's the thing with bringing in these new rules, we've probably got to see a greater body of work and evidence to suggest if that's going to occur."

Rucks will be encouraged to leap for the ball more often. (Getty Images: Michael Willson)

Under new rules, rucks are forbidden from crossing the centre line before engaging with their opponents, while umpires will throw the ball up rather than bouncing it.

Apart from the Neale incident, there was also a confusing moment where Geelong ruck Sam De Koning jumped and overshot the ball, but Pittonet was penalised for stepping over the line and taking possession.

"We'll seek clarity on what that looks like," Hansen said.

"I guess we're still adjusting to that. When you're looking at the ball, you're looking at your opponent, that fine line, it's hard not to cross on occasions

"So I think there's got to be some wriggle room where you might give a couple (of free kicks) up, but it's not deliberate. So we'll reflect on that and give our rucks the best chance to learn and express their craft."

AAP

title: Children in camp for IS-linked families speak about attempt to get to Australia
text:
In a dimly lit tent, half a dozen young girls sit on a rug reading brightly coloured children's books.

Among them are classics familiar to most Australian families — such as The Very Hungry Caterpillar.

As they excitedly discuss what's laid out in front of them, their unmistakable Australian accents cut through the din, as well as a sense of sorrow, and uncertainty, about the situation they are in.

"We just want to go back to our countries, to our family [in Australia], and be with our family and like spend time with them," one young girl said.

"But we couldn't because we needed to come back."

The children were either brought to Syria when they were young, or born inside camps in Syria. (ABC News: Baderkhan Ahmad)

These children are among the 23 who tried to leave the Al-Roj camp in north-eastern Syria last Monday morning, only to be stopped 50 kilometres down the road by Syrian government forces blocking their journey to Damascus.

Their Australian mothers had moved to the Middle East during the height of the so-called Islamic State caliphate, which was declared in 2014, then were displaced after its defeat in 2019.

Some of the children were born in Australia and brought to Syria with their parents.

But for the children born here, refugee camps like Al-Roj are the only homes they have known.

The ABC has chosen not to identify the children.

Children dream of toys and ice cream in Australia

The brief glimpse at life outside the camp fences is a vivid memory.

"We saw trees and all of these nice plants and animals … that was the first time we saw a house," the young girl said.

Another, aged around six and clutching a bright pink, fluffy handbag, described the mixture of emotions of the past fortnight — from learning the group was leaving Al-Roj, to being forced to return.

"Sad and then nice, sad and then nice, sad and then ugly," she said.

"I want to taste the ice cream [in Australia] and buy some toys."

The Al-Roj camp houses people who were displaced by the fall of Islamic State. (ABC News: Baderkhan Ahmad)

The girls' brothers spend time kicking a football in the camp — Al-Roj's gravel roads their makeshift pitch.

Last week, the ABC revealed that Al-Roj staff had been quick to dismantle some of the tents the families had been living in.

When the group returned, they found their living quarters had been taken down — and had to bunk in together.

"The families were in a state of despair [when they were turned back to Al-Roj]," said the director of the camp, Hakmiyeh Ibrahim.

"They were so shocked they couldn't even get out of the cars."

She said the families had been in this camp for more than five years "waiting for this moment".

"And suddenly, after they left the camp and reached it, almost … it wasn't working out. They had to go back to where they came from," she said.

Mothers fear for wellbeing of children in Syrian camp

Their case, along with that of the 11 women travelling with them, has sparked a fierce political debate in Australia.

The Australian government has conceded it can do little to stop the group from travelling home, if they make it out of the camp — with the exception of issuing a temporary exclusion order (TEO) against one of the women, barring her from entering Australia for up to two years.

The ABC has revealed the identities of the 11 women, but it is not clear which is subject to the travel ban.

The federal government also insists it will do nothing to aid their mission, with the exception of what it is legally obliged to do — such as issue passports.

Kurdish officials have suggested those public comments could hamper the process for them to return to Australia.

Two of the children's mothers, who did not want to be identified, told the ABC they simply wanted their children to be safe.

They expressed frustration at how the situation was affecting their children.

Albanese's warning to ISIS-linked families in Syria Prime Minister Anthony Albanese insists the federal government will not repatriate the Australian families with links to Islamic State fighters who remain in Syria.

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has been scathing in his assessment of the women's actions, saying he has nothing but "contempt" for them and criticised them for putting their children in danger.

"We've said that we have compassion for the children involved, but that others who chose to travel to that area have made those decisions in life," he said when asked about the case again this morning.

Earlier this week, The Guardian reported some of the mothers would be prepared to let their children be repatriated to Australia without them.

But the two mothers also told the ABC the children felt safe and protected when with them.

When asked this morning whether Australia would consider accepting the children without their parents, Deputy Prime Minister Richard Marles said: "Australia is not facilitating the return of any of this cohort — it's as simple as that."

'Deplorable' conditions

While Al-Roj camp remains in the control of Kurdish authorities, the Al-Hol camp further south is now in territory held by Syrian government forces.

Some of the Australian women and children currently trying to leave Al-Roj had been living in the other camp for a number of years prior to moving.

The Syrian government has condemned the actions of the Kurds in the management of Al-Hol and accused the Kurdish armed forces, known as the Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF), of allowing a "mass escape" of Islamic State families and associates in late January.

Dozens of women and children seen inside Al-Hol camp on January 25, 2026. (Getty Images: Abdulmonam Eassa)

During a press conference in Damascus, Syrian Interior Ministry spokesperson Noureddine al-Baba said the SDF had abandoned their posts at the camp six hours before Syrian government forces swept through the area.

He said they failed to coordinate their withdrawal, which allowed people to flee through more than 130 points along a 17-kilometre stretch of the camp's perimeter fence.

"This led to a state of chaos that almost threatened public security in the area," Mr al-Baba said.

"Had it not been for the swift intervention of Syrian state institutions, the site would not have been brought under control."

It is not clear exactly how many people escaped. At one point around 23,500 people lived in Al-Hol, with roughly 6,500 of them foreigners from 44 different countries.

Raqqa locals hope Syria's wars are finally over Syrian government forces have wrested back control of vast parts of its territory in recent weeks, as a new president moves to assert control over the war-torn country.

Mr al-Baba said 70 per cent of the population were women, children and the elderly, and he argued the situation discovered by Syrian forces inside the camp was "shocking".

"The deplorable humanitarian and service conditions made it more akin to a forced detention camp than a humanitarian camp," he said.

"We found thousands of people detained for years under harsh conditions, violating fundamental principles of justice and human rights."

Camp urges compassion for 'children who were victims'

The Syrian government has ordered Al-Hol to close, and has transferred those still in the camp to a new facility outside Aleppo.

The ABC asked Mr al-Baba whether any Australians were in that group.

"Out of respect for the privacy of those previously detained in Al-Hol camp under inhumane and degrading conditions, we refrain from disclosing data regarding numbers and nationalities," he replied.

"However, we affirm that everyone will, God willing, receive humane treatment befitting human dignity and human rights.

"In the event that non-Syrian nationals are under our care, we will contact their respective foreign ministries through the Syrian Ministry of Foreign Affairs."

Hakmiyeh Ibrahim says the children are not responsible for the situation they are in. (ABC News: Heidi Pett)

Ms Ibrahim does not think the situation that unfolded at Al-Hol, which she described as a "breeding ground for ISIS", will be repeated at Al-Roj.

But she said it would be in the children's best interests for Australia to bring them home.

"These are children who were victims of their parents," she said.

"They saw nothing in life. They have acquired aggression and harshness due to the environment they live in. They are not responsible for that.

"If each country comes and takes its families, these children can go to rehabilitation centres, and the mothers can also go to rehabilitation centres."

title: Hockey star admits men's team 'should've reacted differently' to Trump call
text:
The captain of the gold-medal-winning US women's ice hockey team hopes people learn from her male counterparts' reaction to their phone call with president Donald Trump.

The men's team was recorded in their locker room on a phone call laughing after the US president invited them to the State of the Union and the White House, adding "we're going to have to bring the women's team" because he "probably would be impeached" if he did not.

While one team member responded "absolutely" to inviting the women's team, it was drowned out by laughter from much of the rest of the team, leading to criticism of Trump and the male players.

Loading...

The women's team turned down the invitation due to scheduling difficulties, although Trump said during his State of the Union address on Wednesday they "will soon be coming to the White House".

Players have started returning to their NHL and PWHL teams and have been asked about the comments from the president and the reaction in the locker room, with both teams touting the strength of the connection between the men's and women's players.

Women's captain Hilary Knight told ESPN there was genuine reciprocal support between the sides that was "being overshadowed by a quick lapse".

"I think the guys were in a tough spot, so it's a shame that this storyline and narrative has blown up and [is] overshadowing that connection and genuine interest in one another and cheering one another on … and supporting each other on the world stage," said Knight, who broke the US record for goals and appearances during her fifth Winter Olympic Games.

"I think this is a really good learning point to focus on how we talk about women, not only in sport but in [any] industry, women aren't less than and our achievements shouldn't be overshadowed."

Men's goalie Jeremy Swayman says the team "should've reacted differently". (Getty Images: Bruce Bennett)

Back-up men's goalkeeper Jeremy Swayman admitted the team handled it poorly.

"Yeah, we should've reacted differently," he said.

"We know that we are so excited for the women's team, we have so much respect for the women's team, and to share that gold medal with them is something that we'll forever be grateful for."

Women's forward Kelly Pannek suggested her beef was not with the men's players with whom they had bonded during the Games, but with the man who made the comment in the first place.

"The video is what it is; you'd have to ask them [the men's team] their feelings on it," she said.

"With the phone call specifically, it's not surprising, to be frank. So I don't know why we expect differently.

"But for us it's also about getting back to the focus on our team and what our team accomplished."

title: Gas giant launches legal action against Australia's biggest pork producer
text:
A major energy company is taking Australia's largest pork producer to court over plans to build gas pipelines through its property on Queensland's Western Downs.

Arrow Energy — a joint venture between Shell and PetroChina — has made an application to the Land Court of Queensland in relation to Sunpork's Tong Park piggery at Kogan, west of Brisbane.

Arrow Energy was granted state and federal approval to build three gas pipelines underneath the property as part of its broader operations in the Surat Basin.

Under Queensland law, landholders cannot refuse gas development on their property, but companies must negotiate an agreement with landowners before carrying out activities likely to have more than a minor impact.

If the parties involved do not agree, the matter can be decided by the Land Court.

Arrow Energy's application centres on a "conduct and compensation agreement for mining activities", but the exact details of the dispute are not clear.

An Arrow spokesperson said it would not comment because the matter was before the court.

Sunpork has been contacted for comment.

Tong Park is one of Queensland's biggest piggeries, with a capacity of 142,000 standard pig units — a measure for a pig weighing an average of 40 kilograms.

In March 2025, the Cameron family formally took over full ownership of Sunpork — marking the end of a 40-year partnership between the Cameron, Hall and McLean families.

Coal seam gas heartland

On the Western Downs, there are hundreds of coal seam gas wells crisscrossing farming land. (Supplied: Google Earth)

Two of the proposed 17-kilometre pipelines would carry coal seam gas and "produced water" — groundwater that comes to the surface during gas extraction — to Arrow's facilities.

Meanwhile, a third, 59km pipeline would transport treated water from third-party sites to Arrow's operations.

The Surat Basin has seen rapid growth in coal seam gas since 2010, and Arrow is one of the biggest operators in the region.

It drills wells, extracts coal seam gas (CSG), liquefies it and exports most of it overseas.

Gas is now the largest type of resource development in the region, with about 9,000 production wells stretching from Rolleston in the north to Surat in the south-west and Dalby in the south-east.

By 2030, that number is expected to reach about 22,000 at the peak of development.

There have been several clashes between Queensland's farming and energy sectors.

Ken Cameron, a key figure in the family pork business, has previously spoken out against certain resources projects.

Ken Cameron was a vocal opponent of Glencore's proposal to store liquid carbon dioxide underground in the region. (ABC News: Nathan Morris)

He joined industry group Agforce in publicly opposing a proposal by mining giant Glencore to store liquid carbon dioxide underground 10km from one of his piggeries.

In 2024, then Queensland premier Steven Miles moved to ban carbon capture and storage projects in the Queensland section of the Great Artesian Basin.

The matter is listed for review in May.

title: Bill Gates speaks 'candidly' to staff about relationship with Epstein
text:
Bill Gates has apologised to the staff of his philanthropic foundation over his connection to the late convicted paedophile Jeffrey Epstein.

During a Gates Foundation town hall meeting, the Microsoft co-founder "took responsibility for his actions", a statement from his foundation said.

" Bill spoke candidly, addressing several questions in detail. "

Multiple emails between Mr Gates and Epstein have been released by the US Justice Department as part of the Epstein files investigation. Mr Gates's presence in the files does not indicate wrongdoing, and he has denied improper conduct relating to Epstein.

According to The Wall Street Journal, Mr Gates told staff: "I apologise to other people who are drawn into this because of the mistake that I made.

"I did nothing illicit. I saw nothing illicit," the Journal report said.

Documents released in the Epstein files include emails about philanthropic work, calendar entries for meetings and photos of Mr Gates at events and with women whose faces have been redacted.

According to the newspaper, Mr Gates told foundation staff that Epstein had asked him to take photos with his assistants after their meetings.

"To be clear, I never spent any time with victims, the women around him," Mr Gates said, according to the report.

Bill Gates said Epstein asked him to take photos with assistants after their meetings. (Supplied: House Oversight Committee Democrats)

Separately, a spokesperson for Mr Gates previously told the ABC: "Mr. Gates never visited Epstein's island, never attended parties with him, and had no involvement in any illegal activities associated with Epstein."

Melinda Gates reacts to Epstein files Melinda French Gates says her ex-husband, Bill Gates, needs to answer for his behaviour alleged in the latest batch of Epstein files.

Earlier this month, Mr Gates told Nine News: "I regret every minute I spent with him."

That interview came soon after Melinda French Gates, who left the Gates Foundation in 2024 to focus on her own philanthropy, said her ex-husband must "answer to those things" when asked about his ties to Epstein.

There has been increased scrutiny on Mr Gates following the release of files pertaining to him. Last week, he pulled out of delivering the keynote address at the India AI Impact Summit in New Delhi "to ensure the focus remains on the AI Summit's key priorities".

ABC/wires

title: Concerns Medicare changes obstruct rural patients' mental health care
text:
When the COVID pandemic began, Sarah*, a country Victorian vet, urgently needed mental health support.

Her demanding job led her to seek help for depression and burnout.

"There was a combination of some underlying depression as well, and in the last few years I've just, every now and then, intermittently just reached out to a psychologist … for support", she said.

At the time, Sarah took advantage of an option to see a GP via a video consultation, to obtain a Mental Health Treatment Plan, which in turn referred her to an online psychological service.

Medicare now requires a patient see a GP in person at least once in the past 12 months. (Pexels: Alex Green / License)

Since November, Medicare now requires patients to see a GP in person at least once in the previous 12 months in order to access telehealth mental health care.

Sarah said this was challenging for her, but she had concerns about what this change meant for others in rural Victoria.

"If someone's feeling really vulnerable or if they're quite antisocial, the last thing they really want to do is go into a GP office or reception where there's other people in town that they probably don't want to see," she said.

Changes are distressing for patients

Clinical Psychologist Olivia Clayton, director of an online practice supporting rural and remote Australians, says the change has disrupted longstanding care.

Some of her clients have been consulting the same GP via video for years for treatment plans, medication reviews, and referrals.

"It is particularly difficult given that our GPs work closely within the same team as their psychologist, providing a level of coordinated, high-quality care that patients greatly value," she said.

Olivia Clayton says the Medicare change is distressing for regional patients. (ABC News: Philippe Perez)

Ms Clayton said the rule disproportionately affected rural patients, who often faced six-to-eight-week waits, travel long distances, and had privacy concerns in small towns.

Access to GPs with mental health training can also be limited locally.

Laura Carter, a mental-health-trained GP also based in Mansfield, Victoria, consults in person and via telehealth with patients nationwide, including some whose nearest GP is hundreds of kilometres away.

She said continuity of care, not physical proximity, determined quality.

"When you've seen someone over multiple video consultations, you can conduct very thorough mental health assessments," Dr Carter said.

"If a physical examination is required, that can be arranged locally. But video consultation is entirely appropriate for developing treatment plans, monitoring medications, and providing referrals."

Laura Carter says high-quality mental health care can be undertaken over video. (ABC News: Philippe Perez)

Telehealth essential for disaster recovery

Australian Association of Psychologists executive director Tegan Carrison said she was "deeply concerned" about what the rule change meant for regional communities.

"Access to things like telehealth for referrals from GPs is absolutely an essential part of meeting community health needs," she said.

ABS data shows more than a quarter of mental health patients used telehealth at least once in the last financial year.

Ms Carrison said this spoke to the need to provide mental health services across a range of regions affected by disasters.

Tegan Carrison says accessing telehealth is essential for regional communities. (Supplied: Australian Association of Psychologists)

"We've had floods and bushfires, and our communities are facing a plethora of natural disasters, and we know from the evidence that natural disasters lead to poorer mental health and an increased need for psychological support," Ms Carrison said.

Change to support continuity of care

A Telehealth Post-Implementation Review has found the best evidence for telehealth is when it is part of ongoing care for familiar patients with diagnosed conditions.

The report recommended telehealth be available for the creation of mental health care plans without the need for an in-person GP visit.

It also noted that even if telehealth could increase patients' access, "there were perceived risks of both lower quality of care and lower value services when telehealth was not used optimally."

Ms Clayton has asked the Department of Health, Disability and Ageing to reinstate the exemption for rural and remote patients accessing a mental-health-trained GP.

The Better Access initiative changes include a requirement to see a GP in person to access telehealth. (AAP: Tracey Nearmy)

A government spokesperson told the ABC $8 million in the Health Disability and Ageing portfolio was being invested to expand free mental health care and would establish more places to access free public mental healthcare.

"These changes [in November] align the preparation of Mental Health Treatment Plan telehealth items with face-to-face services to support continuity of care, enhance access equity, and better integrate mental and physical health services," the spokesperson said.

"There are some exemptions to the established clinical relationship rule, which allows patients to access telehealth services without a prior face-to-face visit."

*Name changed for privacy reasons.

title: INXS nominated for induction into Rock & Roll Hall of Fame
text:
With more than 70 million records sold worldwide and three Grammy nominations, INXS took its talents from Australian pubs to stadiums around the world.

Now, the band has been nominated for induction into the Rock & Roll Hall of Fame.

Previous Australian acts that have been inducted are AC/DC (inducted in 2003), the Bee Gees, who were born in the UK but raised and launched in Australia (inducted in 1997), Flea from the Red Hot Chili Peppers, born in Melbourne, and Steve Smith from Journey, born in Sydney.

INXS is on a list of 17 nominees for 2026 including Mariah Carey, Lauryn Hill, Phil Collins, P!NK, Shakira, Wu-Tang Clan and Luther Vandross.

Chairman of the Rock & Roll Hall of Fame Foundation John Sykes said the diverse list of nominees recognised the ever-evolving faces and sounds of rock'n'roll.

"Induction into the Rock & Roll Hall of Fame is music's highest honour and we look forward to celebrating the Class of 2026 this fall," Sykes said.

Music artists or bands become eligible for nomination 25 years after releasing their first commercial recording.

The 10 artists and bands that are appearing on the ballot for the first time are INXS, Jeff Buckley, Phil Collins, Melissa Etheridge, Lauryn Hill, New Edition, P!NK, Shakira, Luther Vandross and Wu-Tang Clan.

More than 1,200 artists, historians, and music industry professionals who make up an international voting panel will have the nominee ballots distributed to them.

Things that will be considered in the selection criteria include the impact an artist or group has had on music culture and their influence on other musicians who have followed, as well as the scope and longevity of their career and body of work.

The 2026 performer inductees will be revealed in April as will the inductees entering the hall under three special committee categories: Musical Influence, Musical Excellence and the Ahmet Ertegun Non Performer Award.

The induction ceremony will take place in autumn in the United States, which is generally from September through to November, with exact dates to be announced.

The global influence of INXS

INXS formed as the Farriss Brothers on Sydney's Northern Beaches in 1977.

The name is a phonetic play on the words "in excess" and was adopted by the band to reflect its high-energy, larger than life sound.

The initial line-up was Michael Hutchence (vocals), Kirk Pengilly (guitar/sax), Garry Gary Beers (bass), Jon Farriss (drums), Tim Farriss (guitar) & Andrew Farriss (guitar, keyboard, percussion), and they make up the Rock & Roll Hall of Fame nominees.

It has been almost 30 years since INXS frontman Hutchence died in Sydney on November 22, 1997. He was 37.

His death was ruled a suicide by the official coroner's report.

He was in a relationship at the time with British television presenter Paula Yates, who he shared daughter Heavenly Hiraani Tiger Lily Hutchence with, born in 1996.

Hutchence also had a high-profile relationship with Australian pop princess Kylie Minogue from 1989 to 1991, with singer and actress Samantha Jade getting the stamp of approval when she portrayed Minogue in Never Tear Us Apart: The Untold Story of INXS.

INXS didn't perform publicly for almost a year after Hutchence's death.

Noiseworks lead singer Jon Stevens later fronted INXS followed by Canadian JD Fortune. He was replaced by Irish singer-songwriter Ciaran Gribbin.

The group hasn't performed live since November 2012 but has been active with archival releases.

And with classic songs like Never Tear Us Apart, Original Sin and Need You Tonight, INXS continues to be a mainstay on playlists the world over.

Speaking to Billboard from Ohio, Andrew Farriss said he thought everybody in the band was thrilled and excited to be nominated.

"I never started writing songs and playing music to be in a hall of fame; that's not why I started doing it — but that doesn't mean I don't appreciate it," Farriss said.

Never Tear Us Apart was voted the #1 song on triple j's Hottest 100 of Australian songs.

The performer nominees to be considered for 2026 induction are:

title: Teen confirmed as first Australian death from tick-induced red meat allergy
text:
New South Wales teenager Jeremy Webb has become the first Australian confirmed to have died from a tick-induced red meat allergy.

The 16-year-old from the Central Coast began having difficulty breathing after eating beef sausages on a camping trip at MacMasters Beach in June 2022.

His teenage friends attempted to revive him, but he collapsed and later died in hospital.

Jeremy's death was initially determined to be asthma, but NSW Deputy State Coroner Carmel Forbes has ruled he died from an anaphylactic reaction to mammalian meat which triggered an asthma attack.

"Without the anaphylaxis, the asthma would not have caused his death", Magistrate Forbes told the court on Thursday.

The teenager's parents Myfanwy and Johnathan Webb had advocated for a coronial inquest into their son's death, with the hope of raising awareness about the rare and potentially life-threatening allergy.

Jeremy Webb has become the first Australian confirmed to have died from a tick-induced red meat allergy. (Supplied: Myfanwy Webb)

In the hearing last November, clinical immunologist and allergist Sheryl van Nunen posthumously diagnosed Jeremy with mammalian meat allergy.

During childhood, Jeremy was repeatedly bitten by ticks while camping in bushland on the NSW Central Coast.

The tick-induced condition, also known as alpha-gal syndrome, is a potentially life-threatening allergy to red meats such as beef, pork and lamb and sometimes in gelatine and fats.

It also includes more exotic meats like kangaroo, dolphin and guinea pig.

Unlike most food allergies, reactions typically manifest between two and 10 hours after consumption, with symptoms ranging from abdominal cramping and vomiting, to severe allergic reactions and anaphylaxis.

'Jeremy continues to make a positive impact'

Speaking outside court, Ms Webb credited the inquest for providing her family with answers about her son's death.

Ms Webb believes her son's legacy will live on by increasing awareness about the allergy.

Myfanwy and Johnathan Webb said they were thankful for the inquest. (ABC News: Jak Rowland)

She told the ABC Thursday's findings would make him "so, so proud".

"During his life, Jeremy made a positive impact on the lives of his friends, family and also strangers," Ms Webb said.

"Jeremy continues to make a positive impact [by] saving lives into the future.

" He made a difference when he was alive and now he's still continuing to make a difference when he's passed, which is really incredible. "

Mr Webb was optimistic the recognition of how his son died would save lives in the future.

"If these changes in public education can help prevent other patients from dying from alpha-gal, then that's all we want," he said.

"I don't want any other parents to have to go through it. It's something you live with for the rest of your life. It's tough."

Throughout the inquest, experts described a previous admission to hospital as a "missed opportunity" to identify the life-threatening risks associated with his allergy.

Magistrate Forbes recommended Jeremy's death be used as a case study by the Central Coast Local Health District.

Only two confirmed fatal cases worldwide

Professor van Nunen told the ABC Jeremy's death is the first documented fatal case of mammalian meat allergy in the country and second in the world.

Professor van Nunen posthumously diagnosed Jeremy with the condition. (ABC News: Jak Rowland)

US-based researchers believe a 47-year-old New Jersey man is the only other fatal case of alpha-gal syndrome.

According to allergists from the University of Virginia, the previously healthy pilot died hours after eating a hamburger at a barbecue in 2024.

While deaths of mammalian meat allergy are extremely rare, 14 recorded fatalities have been caused by medicines containing alpha-gal — the same sugar molecule found in red meat.

Professor van Nunen told the inquest people had a 50 per cent chance of developing the allergy after being bitten by just two ticks.

According to CSIRO research, Pittwater on Sydney's northern beaches had the highest population of people living with the condition in the world, recording 744 cases per 100,000 residents in 2025.

Over the past five years, cases of the historically under-diagnosed condition have increased by 40 per cent every year.

Most of those with the allergy live along Australia's east coast, with the Australian paralysis tick dominating areas from Cooktown in Far North Queensland, right down to Lakes Entrance in Victoria.

title: RBA governor says she does not think inflation is taking off again
text:
Reserve Bank governor Michele Bullock says she does not think inflation is taking off again, and it is unclear whether the RBA should raise or lower the official interest rate from here as the economy starts to recover.

During a fireside chat at Melbourne University yesterday evening, Ms Bullock called for more patience, saying it was tough to make judgements about monetary policy at this point in the cycle.

"We're in a situation where the labour market, we think, is a little bit tight, and inflation is a bit elevated. I don't think it's taking off again, but it's a little bit elevated," she said.

The unemployment rate was 4.1 per cent in January, which was relatively low, and inflation in January was slightly hotter than expected, with underlying inflation picking up slightly from 3.3 per cent to 3.4 per cent.

Why economists hate low unemployment There's a theory that if you have just the right amount of workers on the unemployment scrap heap, you can keep inflation in check.

"We're sort of in this position … it's actually quite a good position, where the economy is sort of recovering, and this is where it's difficult. So the judgements are a little bit more difficult," Ms Bullock said.

"[The economy's] close to balance — maybe a little bit tight — and that's why people have to be patient.

"It's not like we've got a situation where it's very clear what we have to do."

The official cash rate target is 3.85 per cent, following a 0.25 percentage point hike earlier this month. The board is widely expected to raise rates again this year, with ANZ economists today forecasting a 0.25 percentage point hike as early as May.

Ms Bullock said the RBA Board understood that the data it was using to make decisions were either current or from the past, but it used that data as a starting point to look ahead.

"When we say we're data-driven, it's not to say we're reacting to individual bits of data that happened in the past," she added.

"It's to use those data to cast forward."

The governor also gave more details on how quickly things moved last year.

"So, it's probably worth going back to the middle of the [last] year. We've been lowering interest rates because demand was not growing very strongly," she said.

"We had inflation coming down. We were hearing from our liaison program that the labour market was softening a bit."

Ms Bullock said there were signs that things were "heading in the right direction".

Loading...

Then from last September, she said, "everything was pointing in the opposite direction", just a month after the RBA lowered its interest rate for the third time since November 2023.

"Credit was recovering strongly, housing prices were rising, and demand was recovering more than expected," she said.

"Inflation started to pick up, the world economy was looking now actually better than it had been, and forecasts were being revised up.

" I think there was a recognition in February that inflation was too high. "

'Not in a bubble'

Apart from discussing interest rates, Ms Bullock also admitted that the bank needed to make changes both internally and externally to stay in touch with the economy.

January inflation hotter than expected Headline inflation was 3.8 per cent in January, which was unchanged from December, but underlying inflation increased slightly. Both numbers were higher than expected.

"There were a couple of things that we needed to do better," she told the audience.

"One was an internal challenge and debate … and the other was we needed to, I think, engage more actively with in external debate.

" I don't want people to accuse us of being in a bubble. "

The governor has also highlighted the importance of clear communication with the public.

"We're identifying groups of people that are not as economically literate as others … so we've got to find ways of explaining things to them," she said.

"They may not agree, they may not like it, but at least they will understand where we're coming from.

Michele Bullock says she does not want to be accused of being in a bubble. (Reuters: Tim Wimborne)

"I've been repeating and repeating that we're lowering inflation, but prices are not falling.

"It just means they aren't rising as quickly anymore," she added. "For some people, that is quite a subtle message, and they don't quite get it."

The need for economic literacy among young people, especially young women and people coming from culturally diverse backgrounds, is equally vital, according to Ms Bullock.

Unemployment rate remains at 4.1pc in January Australia's unemployment rate remained at 4.1 per cent in January, with 17,800 more employed people.

"There is a tendency for economics to be mainly boys, mainly from private schools," she said.

"I support young women coming up through the ranks, and I think it helps that you can see women in senior positions now."

In Australia, there are several senior female leaders across the federal scene, including Productivity Commission chair Danielle Wood, Australian Competition and Consumer Commission chair Gina Cass-Gottlieb, Treasury secretary Jenny Wilkinson, and incoming ASIC head Sarah Court.

Ms Bullock said the bank was working with schools to encourage "more girls, more people from a range of different, diverse backgrounds, cultural backgrounds, and also from different socio-economic areas, into economics".

"Because we need that diversity," she said.

title: Philippines up for the fight against the Matildas in Women's Asian Cup opener
text:
The last time the Philippines faced the Matildas was during the 2023 Olympic Qualifiers, where they suffered an 8-0 defeat.

It was early in Mark Torcaso's tenure as head coach of the Filipinas, and the lopsided result was difficult to take.

Sponsor and team manager Jeff Cheng then departed in January 2024. His tenure included the historic achievement of helping the team qualify for the FIFA Women's World Cup for the first time.

Now, as they prepare to face the Matildas in the opening game of the Women's Asian Cup in Perth, the Filipinas are seeking redemption.

"Our last outing against Australia sticks out like a sore thumb, the absolute opposite of what this team stands for," former Matilda and current Filipinas defender Angie Beard said.

The Philippines faced Australia in Perth in Asian qualifying in 2023, and will now face them in the Asian Cup at the same venue. (Getty Images: James Worsfold)

Midfielder Jackie Sawicki, who plays for Calgary Wild FC in the Northern Super League, echoes the sentiment.

"That result was obviously really tough to swallow, but I think this time around, our game plan will be much different," she said.

"I think the dynamic of this team has really changed within the last year, and I believe the combination of our newer players with the veterans will create a good competitive environment that will push us to compete against the best in this campaign."

Winning gold at last year's Southeast Asian (SEA) Games has proven the team has reset, and is ready to fight.

Get the latest: Listen to ABC Sport for coverage of the 2026 AFC Women's Asian Cup in Australia, and keep up to date online.

Developing the pipeline

Since Torcaso's appointment as head coach, the Australian has consistently introduced players of Filipino descent during camps in Manila, while also integrating members from the youth national team into tournament squads.

Many of these inclusions have helped cover for senior players outside the FIFA international windows, but they have also created opportunities to gradually build depth and expose younger talents to the senior set-up.

Australia's Mark Torcaso was appointed head coach after the 2023 World Cup. (Getty Images: Darrian Traynor)

While the Filipinas' core defensive line remains intact, questions remain about whether the team has fully progressed beyond its World Cup-era blueprint.

Jackie Sawicki and Sara Eggesvik bring composure and experience in the midfield, but their presence also shows an ongoing challenge: the lack of comparable players that can maintain the same level of control when they are absent or rotated.

The introduction of younger midfielders such as Bella Pasion suggests an awareness of this gap, but integration has been uneven, with consistent roles harder to define. This has been one of the recurring critiques of Torcaso's tenure.

There are also questions about the team's goal-scoring prowess.

Sarina Bolden, who scored at the World Cup, ruptured her ACL in April 2025 and underwent surgery shortly after.

The team made its debut World Cup appearance at the 2023 tournament co-hosted by Australia and New Zealand. (Getty Images: Erick W Rasco/Sports Illustrated)

In a recent interview, Philippine Football Federation (PFF) president John Gutierrez said Bolden's status within the team remained uncertain at this point.

The Filipinas' all-time second-leading scorer Quinley Quezada will also miss the tournament after announcing her pregnancy in August.

Without its top goal scorers available, the team has turned to a strong contingent of younger attacking players.

Names like Mallie Ramirez, Alexa Pino and Jael-Marie Guy have emerged after making an impression with stand-out performances at the SEA Games.

Alexa Pino was impressive during the 2025 SEA Games. (Getty Images: Mark Fredesjed Cristino)

Sawicki welcomed the shift.

"It's exciting to integrate them at a time where there is everything to play for," she said.

"I think these players, especially the attacking ones, have shown a lot of confidence."

One of the more subtle but significant changes comes in goal.

The retirement of long-time goalkeeper Inna Palacios in December has opened the door for a new generation of keepers to step forward.

Torcaso has recently called up six goalkeepers to camp, including first-choice shot stopper Olivia McDaniel.

Real Bedford's Nina Meollo has been a regular presence in recent camps, while former Central Coast Mariners academy player Kiara Fontanilla, who was between the posts when the Filipinas faced Australia in the previous Women's Asian Cup, misses out in place of 16-year-old back-up Leah Bradley.

Olivia McDaniel leads the battle to be the first choice goalkeeper. (Getty Images: Mark Fredesjed Cristino)

At its core, the roster continues to lean on veterans who still form the backbone of the squad, have been tested on the biggest stages and understand the demands of pressure and hostile environments.

The final roster for the Asian Cup is expected to strike a balance between experience and youth, pushing veterans to maintain standards while giving the coaching staff tactical flexibility in a tournament that leaves little room for error.

"The players understand that we're obviously going to a competition that is much tougher than the SEA Games," Torcaso said.

"So now it's about lifting our intensity, our quality, our fitness levels, our speed, to be of another level higher than that and obviously to achieve the opportunity to go to another World Cup."

Facing the past, testing the present

Since Philippines last met Australia, the Filipinas have undergone significant turnover among both staff and players.

New call-ups, integration of younger talent, and evolving roles within the squad reflect a program still in transition.

While familiar names remain at the core, the structure around them has shifted.

The challenge for the Filipinas at this continental championship is not just redemption from a painful result in their last outing in Australia but showing how far this evolving squad has come and taking another step toward qualifying for the World Cup.

Angie Beard represented the Matildas when she was younger, and now plays for the Philippines. (Getty Images: Mark Fredesjed Cristino)

For Beard, the significance of opening the tournament against the hosts goes beyond simply setting momentum.

"I've always been a 'from the start' player, insistent on setting the tone," she explained.

"Yet in our SEA Games campaign, our first game was easily our worst performance.

"We pulled ourselves up from the depths and played four games with our backs against the wall to win the tournament.

"So, I do believe a strong team performance in that first game would work wonders, but being able to rise to the challenge if something doesn't go our way will be the answer."

The Filipinas will arrive at the Women's Asian Cup still searching for clarity but also carrying the belief of a nation behind them — always, para sa bayan (for the country).

If the team can rise to the occasion, keeping the same laban (fighting) spirit that made them Southeast Asian Champions, another World Cup may begin to feel less like another distant dream and more like a possibility earned step by step.

Venice Furio is a freelance journalist and co-founder of Laban Filipinas, a media collective championing the growth and visibility of women's sports in the Philippines.

She is a member of ABC International Development's Women in News and Sport Initiative, funded by the Australian Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade through the Team Up program.

title: 'Absolute trailblazer': Australia's first female MP honoured with statue
text:
A university is named in her honour, her face appears on the $50 note, and now Edith Cowan — Australia's first female member of parliament — has been honoured with a statue in her home town.

Born near Geraldton, 420 kilometres north of Perth, in 1861, Edith Dircksey Cowan won the West Australian lower house seat of West Perth at the 1921 state election.

A statue of Cowan was officially unveiled in the Midwest city on Wednesday, thanks to $120,000 fundraised by the Geraldton Voluntary Tour Guides Association (GVTGA).

The newly unveiled statue of Edith Cowan in Geraldton. (ABC Midwest & Wheatbelt: Chloe Henville)

'Trailblazer' for women

Association president Julie Clark said the idea came to her on International Women's Day in 2023, and the association "decided to come on board".

Edith Cowan was the first woman to be elected to an Australian parliament, winning the state seat of West Perth in 1921. (Supplied: State Library of WA)

"We thought Edith Cowan should be recognised for all the work she has done … the feeling got stronger and stronger," Ms Clark said.

Cowan championed women's rights before and after her single term in politics, strongly believing in the importance of financial independence and higher education for women.

Julie Clark was the force behind the statue fundraising. (ABC Midwest Wheatbelt: Chloe Henville)

She supported the establishment of the University of Western Australia and fought against the levying of university fees.

Her political campaign was also radical for the time, advocating for state kitchens, day nurseries for working women and sex education in state schools.

"She's an absolute trailblazer in what she actually did in her time," Ms Clark said.

Geraldton locals pose with the statue after its unveiling. (ABC Midwest & Wheatbelt: Chloe Henville)

Hometown honour

Ms Clark said it was fantastic to have the second statue of Cowan installed in Geraldton, "where she rightfully belonged", with the first unveiled in Perth last year.

Sculpted by Joan Walsh-Smith OAM and Charles Smith OAM, the piece was titled "coming home to Geraldton" as Cowan was born near the coastal city at Glengarry station.

The statue was installed days before the official unveiling. (ABC Midwest Wheatbelt: Chloe Henville)

Ms Clarke said the statue location, in Edith Cowan Park near the Geraldton Magistrates court, was a fitting tribute to her work in the judicial system.

In 1915, Cowan was appointed one of the first justices of the court, and she helped develop the Children's Protection Society, leading to the establishment of the Children's Court in 1906.

Ms Clark said it was important to honour Cowan not only as a Geraldton luminary, but as a woman.

"Gender plays a big part because there would be only about four women actually acknowledged and recognised by way of statues in Australia," she said.

"So this is very important to happen."

Edith Cowan's grand-nephew Hendy Cowan speaks at the unveiling. (ABC Midwest & Wheatbelt: Chloe Henville)

Family welcomed to ceremony

Cowan's grand nephew and former WA deputy premier, Hendy Cowan, helped unveil the statue, along with Ms Clark.

Mr Cowan said Edith Cowan "deserved" the commemoration.

"I think it's marvellous that the people of Geraldton have made the decision to erect a statue in her honour," he said.

Mr Cowan said Cowan overcame multiple tragedies in her early life.

Edith Brown married James Cowan, the registrar of the Supreme Court of Western Australia, at just 18 years old. The couple had four children. (Supplied: State Library of WA)

At seven years old, Cowan's mother died during childbirth, and when she was 15, her father shot his second wife and was hanged for murder.

He said this fuelled her "great degree of determination" towards charity and advocacy.

"She decided that she would do everything within her power to improve the welfare of women and children," Mr Cowan said.

"And that's what she did."

Mr Cowan said the extended family hoped that her life of charity informed her legacy, just as much as her brief political career.

title: Feel like your job’s gotten stale? Ask yourself this question
text:
Advertisement

BusinessWorkplaceJobs Opinion Feel like your job’s gotten stale? Ask yourself this question Tim Duggan Work columnist February 26, 2026 — 1:37pm

February 26, 2026 — 1:37pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Save this article for later Add articles to your saved list and come back to them anytime. Got it Share A A A

Get workplace news, advice and perspectives to help make your job work for you. Sign up for our weekly Thank God it’s Monday newsletter. In case you haven’t noticed, we’re in the midst of a South Korean wave. From movies to music, fashion to beauty, this East Asian country is only around one-tenth the size of South Australia, yet it’s having a powerful cultural impact across the globe. There is something else that Koreans excel at: coming up with poetically descriptive terms to sum up common feelings, like ‘nunchi’ (the ability to read the mood of a room) and ‘sseom’ (that flirting stage of a relationship when you both know there’s something there, but you’re not officially dating yet). South Koreans have a word for feeling like your job has gotten stagnant. Getty Images However, there’s a different Korean word that captures a particular place that some of us get to at work. The slang word for this is ‘goinmul’ – prounced ‘go-in-mool’ – and it translates literally into English as ‘stagnant water’. It describes someone who has been stuck in the same job for too long, and is beginning to almost rot away internally due to inaction.

Advertisement

The term was originally coined to refer to online gamers who had played the same game for so long that they had developed great skills in it, but were now stuck in a rut of their own making. The same thinking applies to workplaces, where it’s common for some employees to become stagnant in their roles. Sure, they might know how to do their job with their eyes closed, but they also realise that something is missing and that they’ve overstayed their welcome. There are many benefits that come from experience and staying put, but they need to be balanced against remaining still for too long. I recently wrote about ‘job hugging’, the new Australian trend where people are staying in their jobs for longer due to uncertainty about the future. According to the Australian Bureau of Statistics, Australia’s job mobility rate, or the number of people who changed jobs that year, has fallen from 9.6 per cent in 2023 to 8.0 per cent in 2024 and 7.7 per cent in 2025. Add this to historically high disengagement levels in Australian workplaces, and you have the perfect recipe for a growing number of workers who feel like they are stuck.

Advertisement

Now, there’s a simple question you can ask yourself to determine whether you fall into this bucket: ‘when was the last time I learned something genuinely new at work’? This is not about working hard or feeling stressed in your job, it’s whether you’re still discovering fresh skills and new ways to approach your job so you feel like you’re moving forward. Editor's pick Opinion

AI AI is changing the world. What if we don’t like what it builds? Tim Duggan Work columnist If you couldn’t answer that question without having to rewind several years to remember a time when you learned something new, you’re a suitable candidate to be a ‘goinmul’. There’s no point in waiting for someone else to pull you out of this position, as unfortunately, you’re going to have to do that for yourself. There are three simple steps to freshen things up within your current role. The first is to simply recognise that this is how you’re feeling. It can be a natural place to accidentally find yourself after giving a lot to a business, so don’t be too harsh if you recognise yourself in it. The second step is to push your current job as far as you can to its edges. Find new projects, clients, colleagues or opportunities that can help stretch you without having to entirely change companies. Raise it with your manager in your next catch up that you’re open to expanding your skills and show a willingness to learn new things, even if they’re hard.

Advertisement

Lastly, look outside your job and see what new skills you can add to your life. It could be a new language, online course, in-person workshop, or anything that’s going to stimulate your brain in a new area. Learning doesn’t always need to be directly related to your role, but anything that expands your mind is going to eventually reflect on your work as well. There are many benefits that come from experience and staying put in a job, but they need to be balanced against remaining still for too long. The single best way to keep you or your job from turning into ‘stagnant water’ is to keep moving. It can be slowly, steadily, or even frantically at times, but as long as you’re progressing in some small way, you can stop everything around you from getting stale. Tim Duggan is author of Work Backwards: The Revolutionary Method to Work Smarter and Live Better. He writes a regular newsletter at timduggan.substack.com
