title: The spider was huge and leering. Her ‘dying’ husband would have to wait
text:
Advertisement

LifestyleLife & relationshipsMarriage The spider was huge and leering. Her ‘dying’ husband would have to wait Wifely devotion has its limits. So does our ability to digest onions. Anson Cameron February 26, 2026 — 12:52pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Save this article for later Add articles to your saved list and come back to them anytime. Got it Share A A A

On top of his prostate troubles now comes this – a full-blown heart attack. Belts of pain gripping his chest and shots firing out into his jaw and arms and ... Oh, the things he’d yet to do, the words he’d left unsaid ... that pretty Tasmanian girl from 1976 he’d never phoned ... How could it all be over this quickly? At 70, Matt recognised Death was tapping him on the shoulder. “Excuse me, Sir. We have a vacancy at your father’s table. Step this way, if you would.” Photo: Robin Cowcher But then, like a woman going into labour, when the first pangs subsided, he sat and wondered if it was a false alarm; cramp, nerve spasm, an ambush of arthritis or colic ... He sat waiting, hoping it was nothing ... until the wave of pain peaked again. It was real. He called to his wife, “Deirds, get the car. Quick. I’m having a heart attack.”

Advertisement

They began the half-hour drive along country roads to the hospital, with her assuring him he’d be OK and listing the men they knew who’d survived cardiac arrest. This wasn’t comforting. She could only think of three, two of whom were left physically debilitated, while the third had been starved of oxygen and began voting Green.

Things got so desperate, the pain so intense, life so ephemeral, he told her he loved her. She screamed and pulled the car over in a slew of gravel and profanity and before it was fully immobile she jumped out and ran. At 50 metres she stopped and began to shout back at him, “Matt, run. Get out, run...” Still in the front seat doubled over and clutching his chest, he shouted, “Wha...?” “There’s a spider,” Deirdre shouted. “On the dashboard.” “Are ... you ... f---ing... kidding?” “No.”

Advertisement

“Get back in here. I’m dying.” “It’s massive. And leering.” “Leering? Leering ...” he sobbed. “Just get back in here.” “I can’t.” Over the next 10 minutes he was forced, with one stricken arm and a crusty handkerchief, to hunt down and extract the spider. “It’s gone, Deirds. I got it.”

Advertisement

“Really? I’ll be livid if you’re bullshitting?” “I threw it out.” “How far?” Arachnophobia’s easy triumph over love made the rest of the ride to Emergency so heavy with silence Deidre went as far as to turn on ABC radio. Would you drive your partner to Emergency with a huntsman in the car? I would. A tiger snake? Hmmm ... yes. An eastern brown? We may have found the limit of my adoration. Men with chest pains should seek medical help, no doubt. And Deirds and Matt are my friends, so I hope what I’m about to write doesn’t make him come off like a buffoon – though it will and must, as most things written about him do. At Emergency, blasé paramedics probed and a blasé cardiologist explored Matt while humming a Divinyls song. Their funerial ministrations convinced Matt his death was a fait accompli. Until the cardiologist told him, “You’ve got indigestion. I hope you didn’t run anyone over on the way here.”

Advertisement

“Only my marriage,” Matt observed. How had it come to this? Well, when he consulted the internet about his plumbing problems and found some urology-adjacent individual who claimed a diet of raw onions and honey would restore a fellow’s flow, Matt didn’t see it as a pisstake or a mistake – he saw himself as James Cook, cabbage in hand, about to annul scurvy. He put on swim goggles and performed a frontal attack on a 10-kilogram sack of onions, interspersing their individual disappearance with progressively less delightful spoonfuls of honey. Related Article Opinion

Death My father was dying. Why didn’t I speak up? Anson Cameron Spectrum columnist, The Age The ER cardiologist, who had a midlife sideline in swine, told Matt he’d eaten as many raw onions in a week as a Danish landrace on a bender. “I think I pretty much know what a pig is thinking at any given moment,” he said. “But what were you thinking?” It’s a wild claim. Particularly from a man of science. I don’t believe even as famous a pig man as Paul Keating can read a pig’s mind. But the question about what Matt was thinking is relevant. If you’re taking medical advice from the internet, as often as not, you’re taking advice from someone who, if met in the flesh, you’d be giving advice to. Advice such as, “Shut up and get out of my way, you moron.” It’s worth remembering Dr Google got her degree from the Snake Oil Academy and is as knowledgeable on urology as Marie Antoinette on peasantry. Let them eat onions, indeed.

title: Push for new rule after 8 killed in 10 months
text:
A state coroner has led calls for life jackets to be made mandatory following the shocking deaths of eight men in boating tragedies.

Paul and Thomas Eckert, Alan Bottrill, David Neave, Rodney Ingram, Peter Martin, Roger Walker and Victor Kent all lost their lives in South Australian waters between March 2024 and January 2025.

Their deaths have sparked state coroner David Whittle to call for the state to set a national precedent by “introducing an obligation to wear a life jacket when aboard any motor vessel up to 7m in length”.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

After concluding an inquest touching all eight deaths, Mr Whittle urged authorities in South Australia to lead the way with a new policy on the use of life jackets.

Paul and Tom Eckert, who died in a Port Lincoln boating accident. Picture: Facebook

In his findings, he wrote: “Having reached the conclusions I have about the likelihood of survival if life jackets had been used, I am satisfied that each of these people should have addressed the real risks they faced by wearing a life jacket.”

Mr Whittle made a series of recommendations to Transport Minister Emily Bourke, calling for stricter rules on the lifesaving garments.

“South Australia should lead the nation by introducing an obligation to wear a life jacket when aboard any motor vessel up to 7m in length,” he said.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

“This obligation should not be restricted to when the vessel is under way.”

He made further recommendations, calling for adaptations to be made to the boat operator licensing theory and the Boat Safety Handbook to include updates surrounding life jacket use.

Port Lincoln boating victim Alan Bottrill. Picture: Supplied

He added: “I offer my condolences to the loved ones of the eight people who lost their lives in these four incidents.

“It is my sincere hope that highlighting the circumstances of their deaths can bring about positive change and an improvement to the future safety of all those who love and derive pleasure from the sea.”

Mr Whittle’s findings relate to the four incidents on South Australian waters in March 2024, August 2024, November 2024 and January 2025 respectively.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

Father and son Paul and Tom Eckert, 73 and 40, died alongside close friend Mr Bottrill, 71, off the coast of Port Lincoln in March 2024.

The trio were swept away when a freak wave capsized their vessel.

World-renowned professor Danny Eckert, 44, and his 12-year-old son survived the accident after being discovered clinging to a reef in freezing waters some 12 hours later.

David Neave. Picture: 7News

Rodney Ingram. Picture: 7News

The bodies of the three victims, however, were recovered shortly afterwards, with the tragedy unfolding near Spilsby Island, off Port Lincoln.

In August of the same year, two professional fisherman drowned after their 4.8m vessel was found overturned off Kangaroo Island’s Cape Cassini.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

Mr Neave and Mr Ingram, both 65, were experienced boatmen and fished professionally, with their tragedy described as “unexpected and sudden”.

In November 2024, another experienced fisherman died in a South Australian waters.

Mr Martin, known to his loved ones as Pedro, was 77 when he was found in the water at Eyre Peninsula.

Roger Walker, who died after a boat capsized, with his partner June. Picture: Supplied by family

The alarm was raised after Mr Martin’s 4.75m boat did not return on schedule after he set off to check craypots.

And in the final tragedy covered by the inquest, best friends Mr Walker, 82, and Mr Kent, 69, drowned after their vessel capsized off the waters near Beachport in January 2025.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

A previous hearing was told the pair’s craypot rope became entangled in the boat’s propeller, causing it to overturn in the water.

Victor Kent died after the boat he was in capsized. Picture: Facebook

“One thing that arose quite clearly during the inquest was the erratic behaviour of the ocean swells, particularly where they are affected by reef systems which may or may not be visible from the surface,” Mr Whittle said.

“It is the nature of the sea, when we engage with it, to confront us with hazardous conditions and situations which are not predicted.

“Seven of the eight who died, as well as those who survived, did not have time to put on life jackets during the emergencies which confronted them.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

“This highlights the importance of recreational boaters wearing life jackets at all times when enjoying the sea because when emergencies arise, it may well be too late to put them on.”

Following the coroner’s recommendation, a spokesperson for the Department for Infrastructure and Transport in SA, said: “Marine Safety SA, part of the Department for Infrastructure and Transport, promotes the importance of boating safety education and equipment, including life jackets.

“The department will consider the findings handed down today by the coroner.

“No changes to lifejacket requirements would be made without consultation with key stakeholders including the boating community.”
